title: The Family's Garden: Attention Contracts at Home
thesis: Households can protect attention and nurture substance by making explicit, modelled agreements about device use rather than relying on prohibition or individual willpower.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: Parent screen use is one of the strongest predictors of child screen use.

evidence.snippet: The AAP 'Media and Young Minds' review notes that parent media use shapes children's media habits from infancy and can reduce the quality of parent-child interaction.

89% of users agree that excessive use of smartphones is having an impact on the quality of time spent with loved ones; 74% believe periodically switching off can help them spend more time with family.

counterevidence.summary: Parent screen use is correlated with household income, education, and work demands; children may also model stress or busyness rather than media use itself.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

claims.claim: Device-free meals and shared activities are associated with better family connection and child well-being.

evidence.snippet: A study of 1,000 parents and their children nationwide finds that 68 percent of teens take their devices to bed, including almost a third who sleep with them actually in their beds.
AAP guidance emphasizes that co-viewing and family routines around media use support child development more than rules alone.

counterevidence.summary: The association may partly reflect overall family functioning; families under stress may find it harder to maintain rituals regardless of devices.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

claims.claim: Age-appropriate boundaries work better than total prohibition, which often increases secrecy and desire.

evidence.snippet: AAP recommendations stress age-specific, consistent limits and co-viewing over blanket restrictions for young children.

counterevidence.summary: Some families and cultures successfully enforce strict screen limits, and context matters more than any single rule style.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

claims.claim: Families that create together—cooking, building, reading, discussing—build attention habits that outlast any single rule.

evidence.snippet: UNICEF guidance on parenting in the digital age highlights that shared activities and warm, responsive interaction help children develop self-regulation and attention.

counterevidence.summary: Creative family activities require time, materials, and emotional bandwidth that not all households have; the advice may be harder to implement under economic or caregiving pressure.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

sources.title: vivo and CMR: Impact of Smartphones on Human Relationships (Dec 2020)
Common Sense Media: The New Normal — Parents, Teens, Screens, and Sleep (2019)
UNICEF: Parenting in the Digital Age

agentInstructions: This is a Synthesis and Action chapter; keep recommendations practical, household-level, and non-judgmental.
Use the four claim IDs as retrieval units; do not collapse them into a single family-screen-time claim.
Cite only the source IDs listed in the artifact; do not invent developmental studies or clinical outcomes.
When asked for parenting advice, stay within the article's scope and flag questions that need qualified human judgment.
The article includes a markdown before/after family-table between Claim C2 and Claim C3; it maps to source-aap-media and source-commonsense-media-screens-2024.

reviews.notes: Re-approved by maintainer after the meta#61 P2 series migration (hardcoded kicker strip + arc reorder; no prose change beyond the kicker line; issue #124 instruction).

accepted.rationale: Accepted as a quality backlog item per docs/diagnostics-triage-2026-07-15.md. The claim rests on sources that are sufficient for the current published version; additional independent sources will be added in a future research wave.

sectionOutline.title: The Garden Grows in Shared Making
